description:
GOAL: Lock the cross-process atom-table policy for v1. Document the
decision so fz-ul4.19.3 (send/receive deep-copy) and downstream tickets
can rely on stable semantics.

## Decision (user, 2026-05-11): GLOBAL SHARED SINGLETON

The `fz_runtime` crate's atom table (referenced from src/jit.rs:230 via
`name_of()` and src/jit.rs:264 via `intern()`) remains a process-global
singleton across all fz-level processes. All processes share atom ids.

## Why this is correct under the .19 design

- fz-ul4.19's locked decision is "ONE shared address space across all
  processes — NOT BEAM-style separate spaces" (.19 epic body).
- Atom ids are u32 values, immutable once interned. They behave like
  shared frozen literals (code, persistent literals — see .19.5's
  shared-region discussion).
- Cross-process send of atoms is trivial: the atom id in the sender
  message is valid in the receiver. No translation in deep-copy.
- GC of atoms is not needed for v1 (atoms are pinned for the program
  lifetime, matching BEAM behavior).

## Why we did NOT pick per-process (option 19.6.b)

- Forces atom-id translation on every send (`fz_runtime::name_of(sender_id)`
  -> `intern(receiver_table, name)`). Two table lookups per atom per send.
- Breaks fz-ul4.19.5's zero-copy arena transfer: atom ids in a transferred
  arena would be wrong in the receiver's table, requiring a rewrite pass
  or full lookup translation. Defeats the point of zero-copy.
- Clean isolation has no proven value here — atom names are part of the
  program's static interface, not user-controlled per-process state.

## Why we did NOT pick tiered shared+private (option 19.6.c)

- Two-tier complexity (frozen shared region + per-process append) buys
  isolation we don't need. Reserved if `intern()` becomes a contention
  hot spot under multi-thread.

## Follow-up under multi-thread scheduler (not filed as separate ticket)

When the multi-thread scheduler lands (currently un-ticketed; appears in
fz-ul4.19 "out of scope" list as "Multi-threaded scheduler /
work-stealing"), `fz_runtime::intern()` becomes a write-shared resource
across OS threads. Required at that point:

- Either: lock the atom table on intern (acceptable: intern is rare at
  steady-state; lookups by id are read-only and need no lock).
- Or: use a lock-free hash table (DashMap, flurry, hand-rolled CAS-on-
  bucket).
- Or: per-OS-thread intern caches that flush into a shared table at
  yield points.

DO NOT implement now. Note the requirement here so it isn't lost.

## Deliverables

- This ticket is a DECISION RECORD. The deliverable is documentation
  + audit, not code:
  - Audit src/jit.rs and any other use of `fz_runtime::intern` /
    `name_of`: confirm no per-process atom storage exists today (we
    expect: it doesn't).
  - Add a one-line comment near each `fz_runtime::intern()` call:
    `// Atom table is process-global; see fz-ul4.19.6.`
  - Add a section to a project NOTES.md (or similar) capturing the
    decision and the multi-thread follow-up.

## Tests

- A test that proc A interns `:ok`, proc B interns `:ok`, and the two
  ids are equal. (Confirms shared semantics.)
- A test that an atom sent A -> B via send (post-.19.3) preserves
  identity: receiver compares received atom == its own `:ok` literal.

## Out of scope

- Implementing send (.19.3 owns that; this ticket just clears its
  atom-handling concerns).
- Multi-thread atom synchronization (deferred to when multi-thread
  scheduler is ticketed and prioritized).

## References

- src/jit.rs:230 (`fz_runtime::name_of`)
- src/jit.rs:264 (`fz_runtime::intern`)
- fz-ul4.19 epic body — "ONE shared address space" decision
- fz-ul4.19.3 — consumes this decision in deep-copy walker
- fz-ul4.19.5 — relies on shared atom ids for arena transfer

## Acceptance

- Decision documented (comments at call sites + one paragraph in project
  notes if such a file exists).
- Two confirmation tests added.
- One commit referencing fz-ul4.19.6.
title: Atom-table cross-process policy: global shared singleton (v1)
